html:
Erik Trolle, who held properties in Fånö, Ekholmen, and Lagnö, was born around 1460 and died in 1529 or 1530. He was a Swedish clergyman, district judge (lagman), member of the Council of the Realm in 1487, and Regent of Sweden in 1512. He was the son of Arvid Trolle and Kristina Jönsdotter Gädda (died 1465), and the father of Archbishop Gustaf Trolle.

== Biography == Trolle was enrolled at the University of Rostock in 1480, mentioned as a knight in 1485, switched from his ecclesiastical career, and became a member of the Council of the Realm in 1487. Initially, he sympathized with King Hans of the Kalmar Union, but after Hans's downfall, he joined Svante Nilsson (Sture), the Regent of Sweden. On May 18, 1512, he was elected by the Council of the Realm as Regent, but this election was annulled by Sten Sture the Younger at a meeting in Stockholm in July 1512. Trolle subsequently did not actively participate in national politics but became a district judge (lagman) in Närke and Uppland. After the uprising against Christian II in 1521, he fled to Denmark in 1523, but returned in 1527. He was alive on August 27, 1529, but died shortly thereafter. He was buried in the Nynäs family vault at Bälinge Church in Södermanland.

=== Family === Marriages:

  • Ingeborg Filipsdotter, married in Fållnäs on September 30, 1487, daughter of Filip Axelsson (Tott) and Ermegard Eggertsdatter (Frille).
  • Karin Eriksdotter (Gyllenstierna), married around 1512, daughter of Erik Eriksson (Gyllenstierna) the younger and Anna Karlsdotter (Vinstorpaätten).

Children with Ingeborg Filipsdotter (Tott):

  • Gustav Trolle (1488–1535), Archbishop.
  • Kristina Trolle, drowned in Lake Mälaren in 1495 (approximately six years old).
  • Ermegård Trolle, drowned in Lake Mälaren in 1495 (approximately six years old).
  • A son, who died young.
  • Erik Trolle, alive in 1511.

According to the "Personhistorisk tidskrift," sixth volume, 1904, his wife Ingeborg Filipsdotter and three of their children drowned in Lake Mälaren between Ekholmen and Lagnö in 1495.

Children with the first or second wife:

  • Joakim Eriksson Trolle (died after 1537).
  • Children with Karin Eriksdotter (Gyllenstierna) af Lundholm (died before March 12, 1562).

Beata Trolle (died April 13, 1591) at Steninge Castle, married to Gabriel Kristiernsson (Oxenstierna). Ingeborg Trolle (died 1590), married to Nils Ryning, buried in Aspö Church.

== See also == The Trolle family

== Sources == Biography in Nordisk familjebok (second edition, 1919)

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and how later generations perceived them – not least because those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: During the 16th century, Erik Trolle was a powerful figure in Swedish politics and the church. He held high-ranking positions such as being a member of the Council of State and a district judge (lagman), which gave him significant influence over the country's laws and governance.
lattlast:
Erik Trolle was born around the year 1460 and was a very important figure in Swedish society. He worked with both church regulations and the country's laws, which gave him a high position as a member of the Council of State and as a "lagman." A "lagman" is someone responsible for enforcing and interpreting the laws in their region.

During his lifetime, he was involved in difficult political decisions about who should rule Sweden. He initially supported one king but switched sides when the political situation changed. Although he was elected to the position of "riksföreståndare," a high leadership role, his election was later overturned by another leader.

Erik Trolle had a large family and married twice. His son, Gustaf Trolle, became an Archbishop, which is the highest position within the church. He died at the end of 1529 and was buried in a church in Södermanland.
